images.topic: The Binary Signature — Ransom Note Decoded
images.hook: A physical ransom note dissected under forensic light makes the abstract terror of ransomware viscerally real and shareable.
images.caption:
You open a folder. Every file is locked. Then you find it — the note.

DragonForce's ransom note isn't just a demand. It's a calling card. ChaCha8 encrypted. Cross-platform. Surgical.

Windows. Linux. VMware ESXi. It doesn't matter what you run. They built for all of it.

The BYOVD technique used truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys to kill your EDR before you even knew they were inside.

By the time you read the note — it was already over.

#DragonForce #BYOVD #EDRKilling #IncidentResponse #CyberThreat

images.hook: A map covered in question marks and competing attribution pins captures the genuine mystery of unconfirmed threat actor origins — a topic that cybersecurity professionals obsess over.
images.caption:
Where does DragonForce come from?

Malaysia? Russia? An affiliate collective with no fixed address? The truth is — nobody has definitively confirmed it.

And that's exactly how they want it.

In the ransomware-as-a-service world, origin is a weapon. Ambiguity is armor. Attribution delays response. And every day of delay is another encrypted server, another ransom paid.

363 confirmed victims. Zero confirmed state sponsors. Maximum operational confusion.

Don't fight what you can't find.

#ThreatAttribution #DragonForce #CyberIntelligence #NationState #RansomwareCartel

images.hook: A rendered EDR dashboard catastrophically going dark — killed by a legitimate signed driver — makes the BYOVD technique terrifyingly tangible for security practitioners.
images.caption:
Your EDR didn't fail. It was murdered by a legitimate, signed driver.

Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) is one of the most sophisticated and underappreciated attack vectors in modern ransomware.

DragonForce's affiliates deploy truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys — both real, signed Windows kernel drivers — to terminate EDR processes at the kernel level before deploying ChaCha8 encryption.

The driver is signed. Windows trusts it. Your EDR never sees the kill coming.

MITRE ATT&CK T1562.001 — Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools.

This is why signature-based detection alone is dead. You need behavioral analytics watching for processes that kill other security processes. You need kernel-level telemetry. You need to ask: who is loading drivers right before your EDR goes quiet?

Silence isn't safety. Silence is the attack.

#BYOVD #EDR #DragonForce #MITREATTaCK #CyberDefense

images.hook: A social engineer mid-phone call — with a victim company help desk agent on screen — exposes how the most dangerous attack vector is still a human voice.
images.caption:
No CVE. No zero-day. Just a phone call.

DragonForce's collaboration with Scattered Spider — the notorious English-speaking social engineering collective — represents a convergence that security vendors can't patch.

Scattered Spider's playbook: call the help desk, impersonate an employee, claim they're locked out, and extract credentials or trigger an MFA reset. Native English speakers. Calm. Convincing. Patient.

The result? Legitimate credentials handed to DragonForce affiliates. No exploit required. Your perimeter defenses never triggered because the attacker walked through the front door with a valid badge.

The UK retail attacks of Summer 2025 — M&S, Co-op, Harrods, £300M+ in impact — followed exactly this social engineering entry pattern.

You can patch software. You cannot patch a helpful employee who just wants to solve someone's problem.

Vishing resistance must be trained. Callback verification must be mandatory. Social engineering is the attack surface that never closes.

#ScatteredSpider #Vishing #SocialEngineering #DragonForce #HumanFirewall

images.caption: The ransom note was just the opening move. Then the phone rings. images.topic: Full MITRE Kill Chain — All 10 Steps
images.hook: Security professionals will save and share a visually stunning, technically accurate kill chain map because it becomes a reference tool they return to repeatedly.
images.caption:
10 steps. That's all it takes for DragonForce to go from first contact to full encryption.

Step 1: Vishing call via Scattered Spider
Step 2: Credential harvest → MFA bypass
Step 3: Initial access via VPN or remote desktop
Step 4: Discovery — AD enumeration, network mapping
Step 5: BYOVD — truesight.sys + rentdrv2.sys deployed
Step 6: EDR killed. Defenses blind.
Step 7: Lateral movement — PsExec, WMI, RDP
Step 8: Data exfiltration — double extortion leverage
Step 9: ChaCha8 encryption — Windows, Linux, ESXi
Step 10: Ransom demand — 80% to affiliate, 20% to cartel

Know the chain. Break the chain.

#MITRE #ATTACKFramework #DragonForce #KillChain #CyberDefense

images.caption:
DragonForce has 363 victims. They don't need to add your org to that list.

Here are the 5 controls that specifically counter their TTPs:

🛡️ 1. PHISHING-RESISTANT MFA — defeats their credential harvesting and MFA fatigue attacks
🛡️ 2. DRIVER ALLOWLISTING — blocks truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys BYOVD cold
🛡️ 3. EDR WITH KERNEL TAMPER PROTECTION — survives their EDR kill attempts
🛡️ 4. NETWORK SEGMENTATION — contains lateral movement via PsExec and WMI
🛡️ 5. IMMUTABLE OFFSITE BACKUPS — neutralizes ChaCha8 encryption leverage

The attack chain has 10 steps. You only need to break ONE to stop them.

images.topic: The Dragon Is Already Inside The Walls
images.hook: A panoramic, cinematic wide-screen image of a dragon already inside corporate walls creates existential dread that stops executives and board members cold — maximum share potential.
images.caption:
By the time you see the ransom note, the dragon has been living in your network for weeks.

Dwell time. Lateral movement. Quiet enumeration. Data staged for exfiltration.

DragonForce doesn't knock. They don't announce themselves. One vishing call, one stolen credential, one unmonitored driver load — and they're inside. Watching. Waiting. Mapping every system you own.

363 organizations learned this the hard way.

The 364th doesn't have to be you.

Audit your drivers. Harden your MFA. Test your backups. Hunt for the dragon before it hunts you.

#DragonForce #Ransomware #ThreatHunting #CyberSecurity #CISO
